target.title: fix(memory-wiki): accept title wikilink targets
target.body:
## Summary
- Accept normalized memory-wiki lint targets for page titles and aliases, not only generated paths and basenames.
- Normalize lint link checks for markdown extension, leading `./`, query strings, and heading fragments so Obsidian title links do not report false `broken-wikilink` warnings.
- Add a memory-wiki lint regression test covering title, title-heading, alias, and relative markdown targets.

## Why
Paperclip SUP-1603 found that `openclaw wiki lint` reported broken wikilinks for bridged Obsidian notes even when matching memory-wiki pages existed. The linter only accepted generated slug paths and basenames, while bridge-generated pages preserve the original note title in frontmatter.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw wiki lint` produced false `broken-wikilink` warnings for bridged Obsidian source notes whose links targeted the human-readable page title instead of the generated slug filename.
- **Real environment tested:** Real local OpenClaw memory-wiki vault at `/Users/andrew/.openclaw/wiki/main` on macOS, using this patched source branch against the current vault contents.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran this patched-branch command from the OpenClaw source checkout:

```sh
node --import tsx -e 'import { lintMemoryWikiVault } from "./extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.ts"; import { resolveMemoryWikiConfig } from "./extensions/memory-wiki/src/config.ts"; const result = await lintMemoryWikiVault(resolveMemoryWikiConfig({ vaultMode: "bridge", vault: { path: "/Users/andrew/.openclaw/wiki/main", renderMode: "obsidian" }, bridge: { enabled: true } })); const broken = result.issues.filter((issue) => issue.code === "broken-wikilink"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ vaultRoot: result.vaultRoot, issueCount: result.issueCount, brokenWikilinks: broken.length, brokenTargets: broken.map((issue) => issue.message), issuesByCode: result.issues.reduce((acc, issue) => { acc[issue.code] = (acc[issue.code] || 0) + 1; return acc; }, {}), reportPath: result.reportPath }, null, 2));'
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real vault after applying this patch:

```json
{
  "vaultRoot": "/Users/andrew/.openclaw/wiki/main",
  "issueCount": 3,
  "brokenWikilinks": 0,
  "brokenTargets": [],
  "issuesByCode": {
    "missing-id": 1,
    "missing-page-type": 1,
    "stale-page": 1
  },
  "reportPath": "/Users/andrew/.openclaw/wiki/main/reports/lint.md"
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real vault no longer reports any `broken-wikilink` issues. Before this patch, the same real vault reported four title-link `broken-wikilink` warnings for `sources/super-ai-coach-video-script-skill-implementation-plan.md`.
- **What was not tested:** A released npm install containing this patch was not tested because this PR has not been merged or released yet.

## Verification
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`

target.title: fix(compaction): make overflow tuning configurable
target.body:
## Summary

- add `agents.defaults.compaction.preemptiveOverflowRatio` for Pi tool-loop preemptive overflow threshold tuning
- add `agents.defaults.compaction.maxOverflowAttempts` for Pi overflow compaction retry tuning
- preserve current defaults (`0.9` ratio, `3` overflow retries) and document schema labels/help text

Fixes #80638
Fixes #80639

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Pi compaction overflow tuning is now configurable instead of hardcoded: configured values are accepted by the config schema and used by runtime resolver helpers, while unset config preserves the old defaults.
- **Real environment tested:** Harry's Mac mini, Darwin 25.4.0 arm64, Node v22.22.0, local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/80638-80639-compaction-knobs`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx - <<'EOF'
import { resolveMaxOverflowCompactionAttempts, resolvePreemptiveOverflowRatio } from './src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-config.ts';
import { AgentDefaultsSchema } from './src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts';

const config = { agents: { defaults: { compaction: { preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.72, maxOverflowAttempts: 1 } } } };
const parsed = AgentDefaultsSchema.parse(config.agents.defaults);
console.log('runtime preemptiveOverflowRatio:', resolvePreemptiveOverflowRatio(config));
console.log('runtime maxOverflowAttempts:', resolveMaxOverflowCompactionAttempts(config));
console.log('schema preserved preemptiveOverflowRatio:', parsed.compaction?.preemptiveOverflowRatio);
console.log('schema preserved maxOverflowAttempts:', parsed.compaction?.maxOverflowAttempts);
console.log('default preemptiveOverflowRatio:', resolvePreemptiveOverflowRatio({}));
console.log('default maxOverflowAttempts:', resolveMaxOverflowCompactionAttempts({}));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the real local checkout:

```text
runtime preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.72
runtime maxOverflowAttempts: 1
schema preserved preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.72
schema preserved maxOverflowAttempts: 1
default preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.9
default maxOverflowAttempts: 3
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Configured values (`0.72`, `1`) are parsed and resolved at runtime; default behavior remains `0.9` and `3` when unset.
- **What was not tested:** A live long-running overflow failure against a paid provider; validation used the real config/runtime code paths directly.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-config.test.ts src/config/config.compaction-settings.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts`
- `pnpm -s config:schema:check`
- `pnpm -s check:test-types`

target.title: fix(auto-reply/followup): surface billing/quota notice instead of silent drop (#80700)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #80700.

When the followup agent's model-fallback chain exhausts on a billing, quota, or rate-limit class rejection from the provider, the catch block in `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts` called `replyOperation.fail("run_failed", err)` and returned **without** delivering any payload to the originating channel via `sendFollowupPayloads`. The inbound message appeared dropped from the user's perspective, with zero signal that recovery was needed — the worst-case failure mode for an agent loop.

This change classifies the failure message and, on a positive match, sends a single short notice payload so the originating channel receives:

> ⚠️ Follow-up reply generation failed (billing/quota/rate-limit). Please retry shortly.

## Approach

Following the direction sketched in the issue's "Suggested direction" section:

1. **New classifier** `isFollowupQuotaBillingFailure(message)` exported from `followup-runner.ts`. Substring match (case-insensitive) against: `billing`, `quota`, `rate limit`, `rate_limit`, `rate-limit`, `extra usage`, `usage limit`, `insufficient_quota`. Empty input returns `false`.
2. **Inside the existing catch block**, after `replyOperation.fail()` and the existing error log, if the classifier matches and `run.sourceReplyDeliveryMode !== "message_tool_only"`, send a one-payload notice through the existing `sendFollowupPayloads` path with the in-scope `effectiveQueued`, `fallbackProvider`, and `fallbackModel`.
3. **Notice-send failures** are caught and logged via `defaultRuntime.error?.(...)` so they cannot mask the original error or throw out of the catch.

The notice does not require an LLM call, so it succeeds while the provider remains blocked.

## Why this shape

- **No model-fallback-layer changes.** The reporter asked whether the surface should live at `surface_error` in the model-fallback layer instead. Keeping the change local to the followup catch block matches the existing `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` suppression pattern already in this file (line 458) and is minimally invasive.
- **`message_tool_only` parity.** The same delivery-mode guard that suppresses automatic followup payload delivery a few dozen lines below now also suppresses the failure notice, so message-tool-only flows remain silent on this path as they do on the success path.
- **Explicit notice failure handling.** Wrapping `sendFollowupPayloads` in a try/catch with a structured error log ensures a misrouted/unavailable origin can't turn the catch block into a thrown exception that re-bubbles past `replyOperation.fail()`.

## Tests

Adds 6 regression tests to `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts` in a new `createFollowupRunner quota/billing failure notice (#80700)` suite:

1. Direct unit test of `isFollowupQuotaBillingFailure` covering the documented patterns plus the empty/unrelated negatives.
2. Notice is sent for the exact "extra usage" anthropic billing string from the issue logs.
3. Notice is sent for an `insufficient_quota` error.
4. Notice is sent for a `429 ... rate limit` error.
5. Notice is **not** sent for an unrelated `socket hang up` error (negative case).
6. Notice is suppressed when `sourceReplyDeliveryMode === "message_tool_only"`.

Full file suite: **36/36 passing** (30 pre-existing + 6 new). Typecheck and oxlint on the modified files: clean (the two oxlint warnings reported on the test file at lines 473/477 are pre-existing on `process._getActiveHandles`/`_getActiveRequests` and were untouched by this PR).

## Out of scope / open questions for maintainers

- Whether rate-limit vs. billing should surface as visually distinct notices (e.g., "retry shortly" vs. "owner attention needed"). Kept to a single message here.
- Whether the analogous main-path `surface_error` decision should also fan out to followup. Left untouched.
- The reporter mentions the behaviour may have regressed from earlier OpenClaw versions; this PR does not attempt git archaeology and only restores user-facing signal on the current code path.

Happy to iterate on copy, severity tiers, or whether the classifier belongs in a shared error-classification module instead.

target.title: SUP-1563 restore channel responsiveness health
target.body:
## Summary
- Surface real busy/active-run/stale activity state in channel status instead of relying only on connected transport state.
- Record Telegram outbound activity with the correct account id and avoid treating bot-authored Discord messages as human inbound activity.
- Return visible Discord/Telegram acknowledgements when a message is queued or steered behind active channel work, so connected transports do not look silent during busy turns.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Discord/Telegram transports could be connected while Jarvis appeared silent because a human Discord message was accepted behind active channel work. The fix exposes busy/stale channel work state, prevents false Discord inbound timestamps from bot-authored gateway events, and keeps Telegram accounts connected while Discord proof is checked against the latest human message.
- Real environment tested: Andrew's Mac mini OpenClaw install, gateway on `127.0.0.1:18789`, installed OpenClaw 2026.5.3 runtime plus the SUP-1563 live hotfix, Discord bot account `default`, Telegram accounts `chad`, `default`, `michael`, and `steve`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/openclaw-discord-proof-check.js --json --allow-missing`
  - `curl -fsS --max-time 8 http://127.0.0.1:18789/readyz`
  - `sqlite3 -header -column /Users/andrew/.openclaw/tasks/runs.sqlite "select task_id, source_id, owner_key, runtime, status, datetime(created_at/1000,'unixepoch','localtime') as created, datetime(last_event_at/1000,'unixepoch','localtime') as last_event, substr(error,1,120) as error from task_runs where status in ('running','queued') order by created_at desc limit 20;"`
- Evidence after fix: live terminal output from the verifier showed `completionReady: true`, `gatewayReady: true`, `discordConnected: true`, `discordTurnNotStale: true`, `telegramAllConnected: true`, and `latestDiscordHumanPingHasLaterJarvisReplyOrAck: true`. The latest human Discord message `1503542519265361981` at `2026-05-11T23:41:11.062Z` had later Jarvis reply `1503543303164006632` at `2026-05-11T23:44:17.958Z`. Channel status reported Discord `turn.active: false`, `turn.event: "completed"`, and all four Telegram accounts connected/running. Direct `/readyz` after the proof pass returned `ready:true`, `failing:[]`, and `eventLoop.degraded:false`.
- Observed result after fix: Telegram remained operational, Discord answered the newest observed human message, there were no queued/running task ledger rows, and a follow-up `/readyz` check stayed clean after the heavier channel-status probe.
- What was not tested: No new outbound test messages were sent by the operator; live proof used Andrew's real Discord messages plus read-only Discord REST/status verification.

## Verification
- `CI=true pnpm vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts`

target.title: [codex] Add manifest plugin auth requirements
target.body:
## Summary

- Add optional native plugin manifest `authRequirements` metadata for provider credentials, channel accounts, external services, and host runtime capabilities.
- Carry the metadata through manifest registry records and export `collectPluginAuthRequirements()` from the plugin registry barrel so benches/setup/install surfaces can consume explicit requirements plus derived hints from existing `setup.providers`, `providerAuthChoices`, provider env vars, and channel env vars.
- Preserve legacy `providerAuthEnvVars` hints by merging them into the matching derived `setup.providers` requirement instead of dropping them when both metadata surfaces mention the same provider.
- Document the contract and ClawHub install implication, with a changelog entry.

## Compatibility

This is additive and metadata-only. Existing native plugins keep loading because `authRequirements` is optional, malformed entries are skipped by the normalizer, compatible bundle manifests are unchanged, and runtime credential checks still stay plugin-owned.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: plugin install/setup/test planners can read a manifest-only auth plan, including a provider declared through both `setup.providers` and legacy `providerAuthEnvVars`, without executing plugin runtime code.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, Node via the repo toolchain, using a temporary native plugin manifest loaded by the real manifest loader.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx` from the repo root to write a temporary `openclaw.plugin.json`, load it with `loadPluginManifest(...)`, and call `collectPluginAuthRequirements(...)`.
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from that real Node command:

```json
[
  {
    "source": "setup-provider",
    "id": "provider:demo",
    "envVars": [
      "DEMO_API_KEY",
      "DEMO_LEGACY_API_KEY"
    ],
    "setupRefs": [
      "setup.providers:demo",
      "providerAuthEnvVars:demo"
    ]
  },
  {
    "source": "provider-env-vars",
    "id": "provider-env:legacy",
    "envVars": [
      "LEGACY_API_KEY"
    ],
    "setupRefs": [
      "providerAuthEnvVars:legacy"
    ]
  },
  {
    "source": "channel-env-vars",
    "id": "channel:slack",
    "envVars": [
      "SLACK_BOT_TOKEN"
    ],
    "setupRefs": [
      "channelEnvVars:slack"
    ]
  }
]
```

- Observed result after fix: the derived `provider:demo` requirement keeps the new setup descriptor, carries both the new and legacy env-var hints, and records both setup references; the unmatched legacy provider and channel hints remain separate plan items.
- What was not tested: no real secret lookup or credential validation, because this PR is intentionally metadata-only and runtime auth remains plugin-owned.

## Verification

target.title: fix(cron): guard task liveness during startup
target.body:
## Summary

Task registry maintenance now treats cron active-job liveness as non-authoritative until cron startup reconciliation exits. This prevents a pre-start maintenance sweep from marking persisted cron task records lost while the process-local active-job set is still empty after restart.

The change keeps the boundary narrow:

- `activeJobIds` starts non-authoritative after process boot
- `cron.start()` marks liveness reconciling at entry and authoritative in `finally`
- task maintenance preserves only mapped cron tasks while cron liveness is non-authoritative
- once cron start exits, missing active-job state is trusted again

This is AI-assisted.

## Proof

Real setup: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw`, branch rebased onto `origin/main` at `435e1a74fa`.

Commands run after the rebase/conflict resolution:

```bash
git diff --check
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
pnpm exec vitest run src/cron/service/ops.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.maintenance.issue-60299.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
```

Observed result:

- `git diff --check`: passed
- core tsgo: passed
- `src/cron/service/ops.test.ts`: 13 passed
- `src/tasks/task-registry.maintenance.issue-60299.test.ts`: 21 passed
- `src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts`: 63 passed

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Task registry maintenance should preserve mapped stale cron task records while cron active-job liveness is non-authoritative during startup, then resume marking missing mapped cron tasks `lost` after cron startup exits and liveness is authoritative.

**Real setup tested:** Local `openclaw/openclaw` checkout on the PR branch, using an isolated temporary state root under `/tmp/openclaw-pr-80707-proof-*` and the repository's TypeScript runtime via `node --import tsx`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx .artifacts/pr-80707-real-proof.mjs
```

The script seeds 10-minute-stale mapped cron task records with `runtime: cron` and `sourceId`, runs task maintenance once while cron active-job liveness is non-authoritative, then runs task maintenance again after liveness is marked authoritative.

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output from the isolated local state-dir run:

```json
{
  "stateRoot": "/tmp/openclaw-pr-80707-proof-s9f07s",
  "scenario": "mapped stale cron task maintenance across cron startup liveness authority",
  "staleAgeMs": 600000,
  "beforeStartupExit": {
    "livenessAuthoritative": false,
    "maintenance": {
      "reconciled": 0,
      "recovered": 0,
      "cleanupStamped": 0,
      "pruned": 0
    },
    "task": {
      "runtime": "cron",
      "sourceId": "proof-cron-before-startup-exit",
      "status": "running",
      "endedAtPresent": false
    }
  },
  "afterStartupExit": {
    "livenessAuthoritative": true,
    "maintenance": {
      "reconciled": 2,
      "recovered": 0,
      "cleanupStamped": 0,
      "pruned": 0
    },
    "task": {
      "runtime": "cron",
      "sourceId": "proof-cron-after-startup-exit",
      "status": "lost",
      "endedAtPresent": true,
      "error": "backing session missing"
    }
  }
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The pre-start/non-authoritative maintenance pass left the stale mapped cron task `running` with `reconciled: 0`. The post-start/authoritative maintenance pass marked the stale mapped cron task `lost` with `error: "backing session missing"`.

**Not tested:** I did not run the full monorepo `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` locally.

This shows the fixed behavior: task maintenance does not mark a mapped stale cron task lost during the pre-start/non-authoritative window, and does mark mapped stale cron tasks lost after startup liveness becomes authoritative again.

Also ran a focused Codex review against the rebased diff; it reported no blocker. I did not run the full monorepo `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` locally.

target.title: fix(memory-lancedb): add retry mechanism for Windows Docker bind mount sync delays
target.body:
Hey! 👋

This PR hardens the memory-lancedb plugin initialization path for transient LanceDB table metadata errors seen with Docker bind-mounted data directories.

## The Problem

When a LanceDB data directory is backed by a host bind mount, table metadata can be briefly unavailable after table creation. The previous one-shot initialization path could fail during startup, and the first retry version still had two gaps:

- a table created before `__schema__` cleanup could be reopened and used before cleanup was retried
- the bind-mount guidance was gated on `process.platform === "win32"`, which misses Linux containers running on Docker Desktop

## The Solution

The retry loop now keeps the table local until cleanup succeeds, retries cleanup when a table is reopened, and surfaces generic Docker bind-mount guidance after retry exhaustion.

## What Changed

- Updated `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts` to only cache the table after `__schema__` cleanup succeeds.
- Added Docker bind-mount guidance on retry exhaustion regardless of host/runtime platform.
- Added focused coverage in `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts` for transient create-cleanup failure and exhausted retry diagnostics.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** memory-lancedb can initialize a LanceDB-backed memory table, store a memory, and recall it after this initialization retry hardening.

**Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw plugin registration path using the actual memory-lancedb plugin, actual `@lancedb/lancedb` storage in a temporary database, and a local OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```console
$ NODE_OPTIONS=--no-deprecation node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-memory-lancedb-real-smoke.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:** copied live console output from the real smoke run:

```console
OpenClaw memory-lancedb real setup smoke
dbPath: <temp>/lancedb
registered tools: memory_forget, memory_recall, memory_store
embedding requests: 2
store action: created
stored id format: uuid
recall count: 1
recalled category: preference
recalled text: I prefer deterministic OpenClaw memory proof runs.
startup log: info:memory-lancedb: plugin registered (db: <temp>/lancedb, lazy init)
```

**Observed result after fix:** `memory_store` created a UUID-backed memory in the LanceDB table, and `memory_recall` returned one recalled preference from that same temporary LanceDB database.

**What was not tested:** I did not run a Windows Docker Desktop bind-mount reproduction locally. The real smoke covers the actual plugin + LanceDB store/recall path; the focused regression tests cover transient cleanup retry and the exhausted-retry Docker bind-mount diagnostic.

## Testing

```console
$ pnpm test extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  46 passed (46)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 12.34s
```

```console
$ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
```

```console
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
```

```console
$ git diff --check
# no output
```

Fixes #58139

target.title: feat(trajectory): allow OPENCLAW_TRAJECTORY_RUNTIME_EVENT_MAX_BYTES override for per-event byte cap
target.body:
## Summary

- The trajectory per-event byte cap is currently fixed at 256 KiB, so large but valid events are replaced by a truncation sentinel.
- This PR adds a startup-scoped `OPENCLAW_TRAJECTORY_RUNTIME_EVENT_MAX_BYTES` override for both trajectory writers while preserving the existing 256 KiB default.
- The override accepts byte counts and human-friendly suffixes such as `512kb`, `1mb`, and `2gb`.
- Invalid, empty, zero, or negative values fall back to the default.
- The file-level trajectory cap still applies, so this does not make trajectory storage unbounded.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Oversized trajectory runtime events are currently truncated at the hard-coded 256 KiB per-event cap. The after-fix behavior should keep the default cap unchanged, but allow an operator to raise the cap at recorder creation time through `OPENCLAW_TRAJECTORY_RUNTIME_EVENT_MAX_BYTES`.
- **Real environment tested**: Windows local checkout, Node.js v24.15.0, exact PR head `8f7d4e1a6b95c0561a83b0b71e0b6005d449ee87`, using the real `createTrajectoryRuntimeRecorder` path and a synthetic oversized payload shaped to stay under the inner sanitizer limits while exceeding the outer event-byte cap.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Created a real trajectory recorder with `OPENCLAW_TRAJECTORY_RUNTIME_EVENT_MAX_BYTES` unset and recorded a payload with 60 fields of 5000 chars each.
  2. Created a second real trajectory recorder with the identical payload and `OPENCLAW_TRAJECTORY_RUNTIME_EVENT_MAX_BYTES=1mb`.
  3. Compared the emitted trajectory JSONL line size and truncation metadata.
  4. Ran focused trajectory tests, the extension trajectory test file, the previously failing gateway CI files, touched-file lint, core and extension typechecks, and diff whitespace checks.
- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
head=8f7d4e1a6b95c0561a83b0b71e0b6005d449ee87
environment=win32 x64, Node v24.15.0
PHASE default-cap
env (unset)
trajectory file emitted for default-cap run
bytes 558
truncated true
originalBytes 301299
limitBytes 262144
reason trajectory-event-size-limit
PHASE override-1mb
env 1mb
trajectory file emitted for override-1mb run
bytes 301302
truncated false
field_count 60
```

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/trajectory/paths.test.ts src/trajectory/runtime.test.ts

Test Files  2 passed
Tests       20 passed, 2 skipped
```

Supplemental extension trajectory validation:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       10 passed, 1 skipped
```

Supplemental validation for the previously failing gateway CI files:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/server-startup-config.secrets.test.ts src/gateway/server-import-boundary.test.ts --reporter=dot --pool=forks --maxWorkers=1

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       19 passed (19)
```

Additional local checks at the same PR head:

```text
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <touched trajectory/docs files> -> passed
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json -> passed
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json -> passed
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD -> passed
```

- **Observed result after fix**: With the default cap, the event is truncated to a 558-byte sentinel with `originalBytes=301299`, `limitBytes=262144`, and `reason=trajectory-event-size-limit`. With `OPENCLAW_TRAJECTORY_RUNTIME_EVENT_MAX_BYTES=1mb`, the same payload is preserved as a 301302-byte line with `truncated=false` and all 60 fields present.
- **What was not tested**: Filesystem-level I/O performance at very large per-event limits. The file-level cap still applies and prevents unbounded trajectory growth.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- New operator-controlled config/env surface? `Yes` - `OPENCLAW_TRAJECTORY_RUNTIME_EVENT_MAX_BYTES` controls only the per-event trajectory recording cap at recorder creation time.
- **Risk + mitigation**: A very high cap can increase per-event trajectory disk usage. The existing file-level trajectory cap remains in force, invalid values fall back to the 256 KiB default, and the override is resolved once from the recorder environment rather than reread from ambient process state per event.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows
- Runtime: Node.js v24.15.0
- PR head: `8f7d4e1a6b95c0561a83b0b71e0b6005d449ee87`

### Steps

1. Record the same oversized trajectory event with the override unset.
2. Record it again with `OPENCLAW_TRAJECTORY_RUNTIME_EVENT_MAX_BYTES=1mb`.
3. Inspect the resulting trajectory JSONL lines.

### Expected

- Default cap: event is replaced by a truncation sentinel with `limitBytes=262144`.
- 1 MiB override: event is preserved in full and is not marked truncated.

### Actual

- Default cap emitted a 558-byte truncation sentinel.
- 1 MiB override emitted the full 301302-byte event with all 60 fields present.

## Review scope clarification

The current head resolves the cap from the recorder's explicit environment at recorder creation time:

```ts
const eventMaxBytes = resolveTrajectoryRuntimeEventMaxBytes(env);
```

The truncate helper receives that resolved `eventMaxBytes` value instead of rereading process-wide environment state. This keeps the override startup-scoped and aligned with the recorder's already resolved environment.

target.title: fix(gateway): reduce WebChat ingress latency
target.body:
## Summary
- Persist WebChat user turns to the active session JSONL immediately after `chat.send` ACK and before agent dispatch enters the serialized session lane.
- Pass an entry-scoped suppression marker through reply options, queued followups, and the embedded Pi runner so Pi does not append the same current user turn again.
- Handle session rollover, stale session-store cache, hooks, reset commands, active same-session runs, and trailing user history repair without changing normal assistant persistence.

## Verification
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/web/webchat.md src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard-wrapper.ts src/auto-reply/get-reply-options.types.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-run-params.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/types.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat-user-transcript-persistence.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.overflow-compaction.loop.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `corepack pnpm check:changed`
- `corepack pnpm build`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: WebChat direct messages submitted through the gateway could wait behind a stuck or cleaning same-session agent lane before the user turn was written to the active session JSONL. In the reproduced case, the message arrived through the ClawStation WebSocket path around 22:10 but was not visible through WebChat history until about 22:13.
- Real environment tested: Windows local OpenClaw gateway with the existing ClawStation configuration, direct/private WebChat delivery from ClawStation to an OpenClaw agent, local session JSONL storage, and the user's configured model/provider setup. Runtime logs and private identifiers were redacted before inclusion here.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built and started the patched local OpenClaw gateway, sent a private message from ClawStation to an agent, watched gateway/session logs, checked WebChat visibility, then repeated after triggering the session rollover edge case that previously logged `ignored eager user transcript suppression after session rollover`.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted runtime log / copied live output from the local setup:
```text
before: websocket receive ~=22:10, lane diagnostic active=1 queued=1 last=run:completed, user transcript append ~=22:13:07, chat.history visible ~=22:13:16
after: ClawStation private message submitted through WebSocket, WebChat displayed the user turn immediately after gateway ACK, before the agent lane finished; no duplicate user turn appeared in OpenClaw WebUI
rollover retest: eager user transcript was written to the active rolled session, Pi suppression matched the persisted entry id, and the agent response continued normally
health check while preparing PR: Invoke-RestMethod http://127.0.0.1:18790/health -> {"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```
- Observed result after fix: The user turn is visible in WebChat as soon as the gateway eagerly appends it to the active session transcript, instead of waiting for the serialized agent lane. The Pi runner does not append the same current user turn a second time, reset commands are not written as durable user transcript entries, hook-gated paths still use fallback persistence, and session rollover uses the active session rather than the stale pre-rollover session.
- What was not tested: I did not test a freshly built `main` Control UI against the user's running 5.7 gateway because the gateway protocol versions differ; that combination previously produced a protocol mismatch. The PR branch was validated with targeted gateway/Pi tests and `pnpm build` instead.

## AI-assisted disclosure
- This PR was developed with Codex assistance. I reviewed the code paths manually, ran the verification above, and understand the change.
- I attempted the repository-requested local Codex review with `codex review --base origin/main`, `codex review --uncommitted`, and `codex review --commit HEAD`; each attempt timed out locally without producing findings.

target.title: Signal: match allowlist on either uuid or e164 alias
target.body:
## Summary

`channels.signal.allowFrom` silently breaks for previously-approved senders the moment signal-cli learns the number ↔ uuid mapping for that contact. The pairing flow re-fires for every inbound message even though the user is on the allowlist — just under the other identity form.

This patch makes `isSignalSenderAllowed` treat `<sourceNumber>` and `<sourceUuid>` as aliases of the same identity when both are present in the inbound envelope, so an entry approved as one form keeps matching after the other becomes available.

### Reproduction

1. Configure Signal channel with `dmPolicy: "pairing"` (the default per `docs/channels/signal.md`). Account is fresh — signal-cli has only the user's UUID, not their E.164.
2. User DMs the bot. They get a pairing code. Operator runs `openclaw pairing approve signal <code>`. The approval lands in `<credentialsDir>/signal-default-allowFrom.json` as `uuid:<id>`. Replies work.
3. Later, configure a cron / scheduled task that sends to the same user **by E.164**. signal-cli resolves the number → uuid via Signal's discovery service and caches the mapping in `<configDir>/data/<accountId>.d/`.
4. The user replies again. signal-cli's SSE event now carries **both** `sourceNumber` and `sourceUuid`. `resolveSignalSender` (current code) returns `kind: "phone"` and discards the UUID. `isSignalSenderAllowed` only matches phone-vs-phone or uuid-vs-uuid, so the existing `uuid:<id>` allowlist entry no longer matches → pairing prompt fires.

The user looks "unpaired" but nothing on the bot side actually changed — only signal-cli's local contact map evolved.

### Fix

`extensions/signal/src/identity.ts`:

- `SignalSender` gains an optional `aliases?: { e164?: string; uuid?: string }` field. The discriminant (`kind`) and primary id (`raw`/`e164`) are unchanged, so all routing helpers (`resolveSignalRecipient`, `resolveSignalPeerId`, `formatSignalSenderId`) keep current behavior.
- `resolveSignalSender` populates `aliases.uuid` when both `sourceNumber` and `sourceUuid` are present on the envelope.
- `isSignalSenderAllowed` extracts both possible identities (`senderE164`, `senderUuid`) once, then matches each parsed allow entry against either form.

No other call site needs changes — they all consume the discriminant or one of the existing format helpers.

### Why I think this is the right shape

- Stays inside the Signal extension — no SDK or core edits, no contract changes.
- Backwards-compatible at the type level: `aliases` is optional. Existing call sites that construct `SignalSender` literally (only `core.test.ts` does this in this repo) keep compiling.
- Keeps the daemon-side identity contract narrow: signal-cli already emits both forms when it knows them; we just stop throwing one away on the receiving end.
- Does not change the form pairing approvals are stored under — that stays whatever the inbound's primary id was at approval time. The patch only fixes lookup.

### Out of scope (followups worth considering)

- Storing both forms at pairing-approve time when both are known, so the side-file is symmetric. That's a `pairing/pairing-store.ts` change with a wider blast radius and is independent of the lookup fix here.
- A daemon-level health check that detects "daemon up, no registered account" — separate gap I noticed while debugging this (`extensions/signal/src/probe.ts:24` and `sse-reconnect.ts:48` will both report healthy against an account-less signal-cli). Worth its own PR.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts` — 38 passed (7 new, including the regression scenario).
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/signal` — 214 passed across 22 files.
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` (core), `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — all clean.
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` on the touched files — clean.
- [x] Verified the regression on `openclaw/main` (`64b1f5fbf4`): the new test `matches a phone-primary sender against a uuid allow entry via the alias` fails without the `identity.ts` change and passes with it.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(messages): add responseFooter and enrich /usage full
target.body:
## Summary

_AI-assisted PR. Prepared with AI assistance against a repo snapshot; targeted validation only. See Repro + Verification and Human Verification for exact coverage._

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: OpenClaw had a native `messages.responsePrefix`, but no matching native footer surface for the normal reply pipeline. At the same time, built-in `/usage full` did not surface cumulative context usage inline, there was no inherited config default for response-usage footers, and successful block-streaming flows could drop usage/footer output when final body payloads were intentionally suppressed.
- Why it matters: Users want one native, name-agnostic way to surface identity, model, usage, context, and cost metadata directly on every reply without manual prompt hacks, duplicate footer lines, or per-session setup drift.
- What changed: This PR adds `messages.responseFooter`, extends the shared response-template resolver for late-bound usage/context/cost/session values, enriches built-in `/usage full` with model/context/session/cost data, adds inherited `agents.defaults.responseUsage`, composes built-in usage with custom footers into a single footer block, and preserves footer/usage delivery when block streaming suppresses final body payloads.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add a new `/usage context` mode, does not change message-tool outbound sends or other non-reply egress paths, and does not introduce generic free-form interpolation of arbitrary session fields; supported placeholders remain an explicit allowlist.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #40215
- Closes #12578
- Related #24014
- Related #17832
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: `#24014` and `#17832` are currently closed upstream, so they are referenced as related context even though this PR implements the requested behavior in those threads. This PR intentionally does **not** claim `#39913`, because message-tool outbound sends remain out of scope.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Response metadata was split across two incomplete paths: prefix handling existed as an early-bound template surface, while usage data was appended later as a separate footer line after payload assembly. That meant there was no first-class footer stage, no inherited default for usage footers, and no reliable composition point once successful block streaming had already decided to drop final body payloads.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no seam coverage for “block streaming succeeded and final payloads are empty but usage/footer still needs to be delivered,” and no explicit composition rule for combining built-in `/usage` output with a configured custom footer.
- Contributing context (if known): Usage/context/cost/session values are late-bound and in some cases only trustworthy after the reply completes and session state is refreshed. Existing prefix-only handling was not designed for that later lifecycle stage.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-usage-line.footer.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-usage.test.ts`
  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-reply-pipeline.test.ts`
  - `src/config/runtime-overrides.test.ts`
  - `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`
  - `src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `/usage full` includes model, context window usage, session key, and estimated cost when available
  - `agents.defaults.responseUsage` is inherited by sessions that do not override it
  - explicit `responseUsage: "off"` remains explicit even when a global default exists, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` clears the override and re-enables inheritance
  - static `messages.responseFooter` values fold with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line to avoid duplicate footer lines
  - successful block streaming still returns a trailing footer/usage payload when final body payloads are intentionally suppressed
  - response prefixes do not leak raw late-bound `{cost}` / `{usageLine}` / context placeholders before those values are known
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The risk is in formatting, inheritance, and reply-payload composition. Helper-level tests catch resolver and composition regressions, while reply-runner seam tests catch the payload-loss and block-streaming lifecycle failures without needing live channel E2E runs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing prefix-template coverage already exercised the shared resolver pattern; this PR extends that surface and adds footer- and usage-specific cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- New optional `messages.responseFooter` config for the normal reply pipeline.
- `messages.responsePrefix` and `messages.responseFooter` now share the same case-insensitive, allowlisted template resolver for placeholders such as `{model}`, `{provider}`, `{identityName}`, `{effort}`, `{input}`, `{output}`, `{total}`, `{cacheRead}`, `{cacheWrite}`, `{context}`, `{contextMax}`, `{contextPercent}`, `{cost}`, `{usageLine}`, and `{session}`.
- Built-in `/usage full` is enriched to include cumulative context usage, model label, session key, and estimated cost when available.
- New inherited config: `agents.defaults.responseUsage` (`off` | `tokens` | `full`). Sessions without an explicit override inherit this default.
- Session-level explicit `responseUsage: "off"` is preserved even when a global default exists. `PATCH responseUsage: null` still clears the explicit override and returns the session to inherited behavior.
- When both `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` are enabled:
  - static footers compose with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware custom footers suppress the separate built-in usage line
  - cost-only custom footers still compose with built-in usage unless `{usageLine}` is explicitly included
- In successful block-streaming flows, usage/footer text can still be emitted as a trailing payload even when the final body payload is intentionally suppressed.
- For streamed prefixes, unresolved late-bound usage/context/cost placeholders are blanked instead of leaking raw `{cost}`-style tokens.
- Added `docs/response-footer.md` documenting config, placeholders, composition rules, and examples.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [optional responsePrefix applied]
  -> [optional /usage line appended late]
  -> [block streaming may suppress final payloads]
  -> [custom footer unavailable natively, or usage/footer can be lost or duplicated]

After:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [early-bound prefix resolved]
  -> [late-bound usage/context/cost/session template context resolved]
  -> [built-in usage line formatted]
  -> [responseFooter composed with or against usage]
  -> [footer block appended to last text payload or emitted as trailing payload]
  -> [reply sent without duplicate footer lines]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node/TypeScript repo snapshot in an offline CAAS container
- Model/provider: mocked reply-runner and gateway paths with synthetic provider/model/usage metadata
- Integration/channel (if any): normal reply pipeline, successful block-streaming reply pipeline, gateway session patch path, and TUI `/usage` toggle path; no live external channel in this container
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "responseUsage": "full"
    },
    "list": [{ "id": "main", "identity": { "name": "Jarvis" } }]
  },
  "messages": {
    "responsePrefix": "*{identityName}:* {cost}",
    "responseFooter": "{usageLine}\n— {identityName}"

}
```

### Steps

1. Configure `agents.defaults.responseUsage` and optionally configure `messages.responseFooter`.
2. Trigger a normal reply, a successful block-streaming reply, and one or more `/usage` mode changes (`off`, `tokens`, `full`).
3. Inspect the final reply payloads and the effective session `responseUsage` state.

### Expected

- Sessions without an explicit override inherit `agents.defaults.responseUsage`.
- `/usage full` includes per-message tokens plus cumulative context, model, session, and estimated cost when available.
- Static custom footers fold with built-in usage into one footer block, while usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line.
- Successful block streaming does not drop footer/usage output just because the final body payload was suppressed.
- Prefixes do not leak raw late-bound placeholders before values are known.

### Actual

- In the covered paths, inherited usage defaults, explicit `off`, and clear-to-inherit (`null`) semantics behave as expected.
- `/usage full` is enriched with context/model/session/cost data in the covered reply paths.
- Footer composition follows the intended static-vs-usage-aware rules, and successful block streaming still returns footer/usage content as a trailing payload.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Representative snippets from the targeted coverage for this branch:

```

```

```

```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - reviewed the touched config/type/schema/help/label surfaces for `messages.responseFooter` and `agents.defaults.responseUsage`
  - reviewed the reply-runner composition path for normal replies and the success path where block streaming suppresses final body payloads
  - inspected the new resolver/composition tests for footer, usage, inheritance, and prefix late-binding behavior
  - ran `git diff --check`
  - parsed changed TypeScript files successfully with the TypeScript compiler API in the offline container
  - manually exercised targeted helper/runtime behavior for prefix soft-blanking, cost-only footer composition, and enriched full usage-line formatting
- Edge cases checked:
  - explicit `off` versus inherited default
  - `PATCH responseUsage: null` re-enables inheritance
  - static footer versus usage-aware footer versus cost-only footer composition
  - blank or whitespace-only footer templates emit nothing

- What you did **not** verify:

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.

No review conversations exist yet because this PR body is being prepared before the PR is opened. Once the PR is live, the author should update these checkboxes based on the actual review state.

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk:

- Risk:
  - Late-bound usage/context/cost values could be missing or stale when templates render.
  - Mitigation: The footer path resolves against late-bound reply/session data, prefers refreshed persisted session state when available, and soft-blanks unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders instead of leaking raw tokens.
- Risk:

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: diag(cli-runner): attribute AbortError trigger source
target.body:
## Problem

`[agent/cli-backend] claude live session turn failed: ... error=AbortError` log lines have no detail field, so operators cannot tell which of four distinct trigger paths fired:

1. User-typed abort word (`stop`, `wait`, etc. via `ABORT_TRIGGERS`)
2. `abortForRestart` from compaction-restart / drain
3. Mid-turn `/model` switch (`requestLiveSessionModelSwitch`)
4. Explicit `chat.abort` RPC

This makes the bug pattern impossible to fix without speculation. We've been seeing 20+ AbortError lines / 10 days across our deployment with no way to attribute them.

## Change

Diagnostic enrichment only — no behavior change.

- `src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts`:
  - `createAbortError(reason?)` now accepts and includes the upstream abort reason in the error message (was: zero-arg, fixed `"CLI run aborted"`).
  - `failTurn` appends `detail="..."` (truncated to 240 chars) to the log line.
  - The two abort paths split: `abortFromSignal` reads `params.context.params.abortSignal.reason` (preserves `controller.abort(reason)` payload — `UserAbortError` for stop-words, `Error("Reply operation aborted for restart")` for compaction, etc.); `abortFromReplyBackend` tags `replyBackend:<reason>` (`user_abort`, `restart`, or `superseded`).
- `src/agents/cli-runner/execute.ts`: same `createCliAbortError(reason?)` upgrade for the non-live CLI path.
- `src/gateway/chat-abort.ts`: `abortChatRunById` calls `controller.abort("chat-abort:<stopReason>")` instead of `.abort()`, so the existing `stopReason` (e.g., `"rpc"`, `"stop"`, `"timeout"`) propagates.

## Effect on log lines

Before:
\`\`\`
... error=AbortError
\`\`\`

After (one of):
\`\`\`
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: AbortError: Reply operation aborted by user"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: AbortError: Reply operation aborted for restart"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: replyBackend:restart"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: chat-abort:rpc"
\`\`\`

That is sufficient to attribute each abort to its real source (operator stop-word vs. compaction restart vs. model switch vs. RPC) without re-investigating from scratch.

## Tests

- New: `src/agents/cli-runner/abort-reason.test.ts` — 7 unit tests covering string / Error / object / nullish abort-reason inputs for both `createAbortError` (live-session path) and `createCliAbortError` (non-live path). All pass.
- Existing: `src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts` 4/4 pass; `src/agents/cli-runner/` 35/35 pass.
- `pnpm tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `pnpm tsgo -p tsconfig.core.test.json` both clean.

`pnpm check:changed` not run — would be appreciated as part of CI verification.

## Out of scope

I deliberately did NOT raise the abort grace timeout, change `ABORT_TRIGGERS`, or alter compaction-restart policy. Without trigger evidence those would be guesses. This PR is the prerequisite — once a few new aborts arrive with attributed reasons, the actual fix becomes obvious.

## Provenance

This change was scoped and authored by a Claude Code background agent during a source-level diagnostic of multiple gateway issues. Full investigation writeup (root causes, failing call paths, what wasn't fixed and why) is in `DIAGNOSTIC.md` on the branch but not included in the diff so it doesn't pollute the repo.

target.title: fix(plugins): supplement ClawHub artifact metadata
target.body:
## Summary

- Accept current ClawHub artifact resolver responses where `artifact` is the package artifact summary shape.
- When the resolver response has a legacy zip artifact but no archive verification metadata, load the version metadata and use its `sha256hash` or `files[]` for verification.
- Add a regression test for the current `clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9` response shape.

## Why

The ClawHub artifact endpoint can return a legacy zip artifact summary while the install verification metadata is only present on the version endpoint. OpenClaw rejected that package as missing verification metadata, even though the package was valid and installable.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts && git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Real-world validation

- `HOME=$tmp/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$tmp/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$tmp/openclaw.json pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9 --dangerously-force-unsafe-install`
- Result: installed `zeroapi-router` from ClawHub in an isolated temp state.

## AI-assisted

Yes. I used Codex and reviewed the changed code and tests.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Installing a ClawHub legacy zip plugin must verify archive metadata using the full version metadata path when the artifact resolver only returns summary metadata.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`, installing the live `clawhub:zeroapi` package.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the real CLI install path from the PR branch: `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi --force`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text
OPENCLAW_HOME:/tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l
exit:0
> openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/doruk/Desktop/Asuman/.worktrees/openclaw-clawhub-artifact-metadata
> node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install clawhub:zeroapi --force

Resolving clawhub:zeroapi...
ClawHub code-plugin zeroapi@3.8.12 channel=community verification=source-linked
Compatibility: pluginApi=>=2026.5.2 minGateway=2026.5.2
ClawHub package "zeroapi" is community...
Downloading plugin zeroapi@3.8.12 from ClawHub...
Extracting ... zeroapi-3.8.12.tgz...
Plugin manifest id "zeroapi-router" differs from npm package name "zeroapi"; using manifest id as the config key.
Installing to /tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l/.openclaw/extensions/zeroapi-router...
Installed plugin: zeroapi-router
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
stderr_tail: [plugins] zeroapi-config.json not found. Run /zeroapi to configure.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live ClawHub plugin install resolved version metadata, downloaded and extracted the plugin, and exited with status 0 in a fresh temporary OpenClaw home.
- What was not tested: I did not restart a full gateway from the temporary install. The proof covers the resolver/install path changed by this PR, while focused tests cover metadata fallback behavior.

target.title: fix: prune all history images when current turn has fresh attachments
target.body:
## Problem

When sending a new image for analysis, stale image data from previous conversation turns can contaminate the model's interpretation of the current image. Reported in #66702.

## Solution

- Add `HistoryImagePruneOptions` interface with `preserveRecentCompletedTurns` parameter
- Thread option through `resolvePruneBeforeIndex` → `pruneProcessedHistoryImages` → caller in `attempt.ts`
- When current turn has fresh images, pass `preserveRecentCompletedTurns: 0` to strip all historical image data
- No new images: existing conservative default (preserve 3 turns) applies

Backward-compatible. Existing tests unchanged.

Fixes #66702

target.title: feat(telegram): add acknowledgement sticker support
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram does not support streaming text, so users get no immediate interactive feedback while OpenClaw generates a response.
- Why it matters: Ack reactions help, but stickers are more visible and expressive for Telegram conversations.
- What changed: Added configurable Telegram ack stickers with scope control, optional silent delivery, optional removal after reply, config schema/docs, SDK feedback support, and Telegram tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Existing ack reaction behavior remains supported; this does not add text streaming to Telegram or change default Telegram access policy.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram lacks streaming text support, so acknowledgement feedback is handled through channel feedback primitives.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/identity.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.ack-sticker.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Telegram resolves ackSticker config, sends the configured sticker before dispatching the agent turn, and optionally removes it after reply.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It covers config resolution, channel feedback shape, Telegram send behavior, and dispatch cleanup without requiring live Telegram credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing ack reaction tests covered reaction behavior only.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests are added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Telegram users can configure `ackSticker` globally, per account, per direct/group chat, or per topic. When enabled, OpenClaw sends a Telegram sticker acknowledgement before generating the response. Existing ack reactions continue to work.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Telegram message -> OpenClaw agent loop -> Telegram final reply

After:
Telegram message -> ack sticker -> OpenClaw agent loop -> Telegram final reply -> optional ack sticker cleanup
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Telegram may call `sendSticker` and optionally delete that sticker message. This uses the existing configured Telegram bot token and existing channel access controls; no new credential source or sender authorization path is added.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22, pnpm
- Model/provider: Not required for unit coverage
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      enabled: true,
      ackSticker: {
        fileId: "CAAC...",
        scope: "direct",
        silent: true,
        removeAfterReply: true,
      },
    },
  },
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure Telegram `ackSticker.fileId` with a sendable Telegram sticker file id.
2. Start the OpenClaw gateway with Telegram enabled.
3. Send a DM to the Telegram bot.

### Expected

- Telegram sends the configured acknowledgement sticker before the agent response.
- The agent response is delivered normally.
- If `removeAfterReply` is enabled, the acknowledgement sticker is deleted after the final reply.

### Actual

- Unit and config tests pass for the implemented behavior.
- Live Telegram verification was attempted locally but blocked by local gateway token/env setup issues, not by the ackSticker implementation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation run:

```sh
pnpm test src/agents/identity.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.ack-sticker.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
pnpm config:schema:check
pnpm config:channels:check
pnpm config:docs:gen
pnpm config:docs:check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed-ts-files>
git diff --check
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Config schema accepts ackSticker fields.
  - Channel feedback carries ackSticker configuration.
  - Telegram send path sends stickers and respects notification options.
  - Telegram dispatch path can clean up ack stickers after reply.
  - Docs/generated config baseline are updated.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Scope resolution mirrors ack reaction behavior.
  - Sticker cleanup is best-effort.
  - Ack reaction remains available and can be disabled independently.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full live Telegram round-trip with a real bot after local launchd token setup issues.
  - Broad full-suite CI locally.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Existing configs continue to work. Users can opt in by adding `channels.telegram.ackSticker` or `messages.ackSticker`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Invalid Telegram sticker `file_id` causes the acknowledgement sticker send to fail.
  - Mitigation: Ack sticker send failure is isolated from final reply delivery.
- Risk: Sticker deletion can fail due to Telegram permissions or timing.
  - Mitigation: Cleanup is best-effort and does not block final response delivery.

target.title: fix(memory, builtin backend): eagerly preload session transcript listeners at gateway startup
target.body:
## Summary (scope narrowed to builtin backend per Codex review)

Eagerly preload `MemoryIndexManager` at gateway startup for every agent whose `agents.defaults.memorySearch.sources` (or per-agent overlay) includes `"sessions"`, **when using the builtin session-file backend**. This arms `ensureSessionListener()` before the first `sessionTranscriptUpdate` event fires, so `/reset` and `/new` archive emits actually land in the listener set and get indexed into `chunks` without the user having to run `openclaw memory index --force`.

## Why it was broken

`MemoryIndexManager` is lazy-loaded on the first `memory_search` tool call. If a user types `/reset` or `/new` before that first search, the builtin `archiveFileOnDisk` emit lands in an empty `SESSION_TRANSCRIPT_LISTENERS` set and is silently dropped, so the resulting `.jsonl.reset.<iso>` / `.jsonl.deleted.<iso>` archives never enter `chunks`. Only `memory index --force` recovers them (which is #76311 / #76452's escape hatch but is not a cold-start fix).

## Why builtin-only (Codex review response)

Thanks @clawsweeper for the P2 catch on the qmd path. The initial revision of this PR treated any successful `getActiveMemorySearchManager` result as listener-armed, but under the qmd backend that call returns the `FallbackMemoryManager` wrapper whose inner `MemoryIndexManager` is only constructed by `fallbackFactory`. So on qmd, merely obtaining the wrapper does NOT run `ensureSessionListener()`, and the cold-start race still drops `sessionTranscriptUpdate` emits.

Three options were on the table (per Codex):
1. Explicitly preload the builtin `MemoryIndexManager` listener owner even for qmd — requires reaching into the FallbackMemoryManager fallback slot.
2. Add qmd-native transcript event handling inside `qmd-manager.ts`.
3. **Narrow this PR to builtin-only and defer qmd to a follow-up.**

This revision takes **option 3**: added an explicit early-return guard `if (params.cfg.memory?.backend === "qmd") { ... return; }` at the top of `startGatewayMemorySessionListeners`, with a log line making the scope decision visible, plus a regression test (`skips preload entirely when memory.backend is qmd (Codex review scope guard)`). The changelog entry is also retitled `Memory/sessions (builtin backend):` and calls out the intentional qmd deferral.

## Independent verification (builtin only)

CarHer builtin-backend deployment (`carher-199` baseline). Three cold restarts, same container, identical `/reset` sequence:

| | pre-preload | post-preload |
|---|---|---|
| archive emit lands in listener set | ❌ dropped | ✅ captured |
| `chunks` delta after 30s | +0 | +41 |
| verified commit | `main @ 9a49d143c819` | `HEAD of this PR` |

## What is NOT included

- qmd backend session-listener arming (follow-up PR, tracked in body + changelog).
- Changes to `qmd-manager.ts` transcript event handling.
- Changes to `FallbackMemoryManager` fallback construction.

Refs #56131. AI-assisted.

target.title: fix(ui): make dreams diary day chips sticky to prevent scroll-away on long entries
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #76638 — when a diary entry had long content (10+ paragraphs), the day-chip navigation bar at the top would be pushed out of the viewport, making it impossible to switch between dates.

## Root cause

`.dreams-diary__daychips` used `position: relative` with no sticky behavior. Long `<article>` content would scroll the entire area, pushing the chips off-screen.

## Fix

Changed `.dreams-diary__daychips`:
- `position: relative` → `position: sticky; top: 0`
- Added `background: var(--bg)` to prevent content bleed-through
- Bumped `z-index` from 1 to 2 to stay above diary entries

## Test plan

- [x] Verified CSS parses without errors
- [x] Day chips remain visible when scrolling long diary entries

target.title: docs(prometheus): warn that plugins.allow is strict-mode
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a `<Warning>` callout to the Prometheus Quick Start clarifying that `plugins.allow` is **strict-mode** — when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load. The current Quick Start example shows `allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` without warning that this disables every other configured plugin on a real running gateway.

## AI assistance disclosure

This PR was drafted with **Claude Code** (Anthropic's CLI agent).

- [x] **AI-assisted** — drafted by Claude Code (this section makes it explicit; trailing footer also marks the agent)
- [x] **Testing**: lightly tested. Empirically reproduced the failure mode that motivates this Warning — `plugins.allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` dropped a 72-plugin running v2026.5.2 gateway to 9 enabled plugins; recovered via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + gateway restart. Did NOT build the docs site locally — render check left for CI / maintainer.
- [x] **Author understanding** — confirmed source-level grasp of the activation flow (`src/plugins/config-activation-shared.ts` `resolvePluginActivationDecisionShared`), the three narrow exceptions to `plugins.allow` (`selected-memory-slot`, `selected-context-engine-slot`, conditional `bundled-channel-enabled-in-config`), and the capability gate at `services-CTQW_M_S.js:15` that motivated cross-referenced #76628.
- [ ] **`codex review --base origin/main` not run locally** — Codex CLI is not installed in this contributor environment. Relying on the GitHub Codex bot (clawsweeper) auto-review run that posted to this PR as the AI review pass.
- [x] **Bot review conversations** — clawsweeper Codex review on this PR addressed in a follow-up comment below (covers the soft suggestion about example tuning).

## Why

Following the Quick Start example as-written on a multi-plugin gateway (e.g. one running `memory-core`, `active-memory`, `memory-wiki`, `telegram`, etc.) silently flips the gateway into strict-allowlist mode and drops all other configured plugins. On a 71-plugin running gateway (mid-sized self-hosted setup), the example dropped the enabled-plugin count from 72 to **9** — including critical plugins like `memory-core`, `active-memory`, and the configured Telegram channel — until rolled back via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow`.

The warning surface for this is misleading in two ways:

1. The startup log line `[plugins] plugins.allow is empty; discovered non-bundled plugins may auto-load: <id>. Set plugins.allow to explicit trusted ids.` *implies* setting `plugins.allow` is the right next step. It isn't, in the general case.
2. The schema description for `plugins.allow` is correct (*"when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load"*), but the Quick Start snippet doesn't reproduce that semantics in user-visible documentation.

This PR adds a single `<Warning>` callout next to the snippet, naming the failure mode explicitly so users on existing multi-plugin gateways don't trip the regression.

## Cross-references

- **#75575** (open) — *Honor `plugins.allow` on runtime provider paths (currently force-loaded) — propose `plugins.bundledMode` opt-in*. Same family of `plugins.allow` semantic confusion. The Warning links to that issue for users who want the longer architectural discussion.
- **#76628** (just filed) — *@openclaw/diagnostics-prometheus loads via npm but internalDiagnostics capability is bundled-only* — describes a separate but adjacent failure mode in the same Quick Start path; mentions this PR's `plugins.allow` finding as one of the empirical investigations along the way.

## Scope

- Single-file doc change: `docs/gateway/prometheus.md`
- No code changes
- No behavior changes
- Backward compatible (callouts are additive)

## Test plan

- [x] Source-level check: `<Warning>` is the same MDX component already used elsewhere in the same file (`docs/gateway/prometheus.md:19-21`), so no new component dependencies. Indentation matches the surrounding `<Step>` / `<Tabs>` block.
- [ ] Docs preview render — left for maintainer/CI; I have not built the docs site locally.
- [x] Empirically reproduced the regression that this Warning describes (multi-plugin gateway dropping from 72 → 9 enabled plugins after applying the existing snippet, fully recovered by `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + restart). See **#76628** for the full reproduction.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
